text: Influence of triplet excitons on the lifetime of polymer-based organic light emitting diodes
Oili Pekkola*, Andrea Gassmann', Fabian Etzold, Frédéric Laquai², and Heinz von Seggern'

1ElectronicMaterialsDivision,InstituteofMaterialsScience,TechnischeUniversittDarmstad,Alarich-WeisSt.2 64287 Darmstadt, Germany 2MaxPlanck Research Group for Organic Optoelectronics, Max Planck Institute for Polymer Research, Ackermannweg10, 55128 Mainz, Germany Received 28 October 2013, revised 19 December 2013, accepted 7 January 2014 Published online 5 February 2014
Dedicated to Wolfram Jaegermann on the occasion of his 6Oth birthday
Keywords device lifetime, excitons,organic light-emitting diodes, polymers *Corresponding author: e-mail: pekkola@e-mat.tu-darmstadt.de, Phone:+496151 166331, Fax: +496151166305

In this work, the harmful effect of triplet excitons on the lifetime of poly(p-phenylene vinylene)(PPV)-based organic light emiting diodes is demonstrated. A model system has been designed in which the triplet population in a PPV derivative is increased by blending the polymer with the triplet sensitizer platinum (Il) octaethylporphyrine ketone (PtOEPK). The increase in the triplet concentration of the polymer affects both the initial decay and the long-term evolution of luminance and leads to a drastic acceleration of device degradation, reflected in a dramatically shortened ifetime.

1 Introduction Despite the progress made in the field of polymer-based organic light emitting diodes (PLEDs) during the last years, continuing efforts in research are necessary for a substantial breakthrough in commercializa- tion. The lifetime and stability of the devices are still one of the main issues in need of improvement [1–5]. Usually, luminance degradation and an increase in operating voltage with prolonged electrical driving are typical features of electrical fatigue in polymer-based OLEDs [4]. Thereby the device lifetime is normally defined as the tso or half lifetime – the time in which the luminance decreases to 50% from the initial value during operation at constant current.

In this contribution, the effect of triplet excitons on the degradation of OLEDs is studied in a PLED in which the polymer is blended with a triplet sensitizer. The presence of the sensitizer leads to the enhancement of the triplet concentration in the matrix semiconductor. The impact of the higher triplet concentration on device lifetime is monitored. As a result, an accelerated degradation due to the increased triplet-to-singlet ratio will be reported.

2 Experimental This study focuses on devices based on the poly(p-phenylene vinylene) (PPV) derivative OC3Cg- PPV (poly(2-propoxy-5-(2′-ethylhexyloxy)-phenylene viny1- ene), which is used both in pristine state and blended with the triplet sensitizer material platinum (Il) octaethylporphyrine ketone (PtOEPK) purchased from Livchem Logistics GmbH. The PLEDs consist of an ITO anode, a PEDOT:PSS hole injection layer (40 nm), an OC3Cg-PPV layer (130 nm) and a Ca cathode (20 nm) protected by an Al layer (100 nm).

According to spin statistics, the ratio of singlet to triplet excitons in OLEDs is 1:3. However, differing ratios have been reported for many materials [6–9] but no consensus on the value has been reached. Nevertheless, due to the high lifetime of triplet excitons on the order of several s to ms [10, 11], high triplet densities are reached in fluorescent devices. The inffuence of these non-radiatively decaying excitons on the lifetime [12] or efficiency [13–15] of fluorescent OLEDs has been investigated in only few publications. King et al. [12], e.g., reported an increase in the t50 lifetime as well as a decrease in the initial luminescence decay in polyfuorene-based OLEDs after reducing the amount of triplets in the polymer matrix by incorporating a triplet quencher additive into the system.
All devices were fabricated and characterized in an inert nitrogen atmosphere. The polymer layers were spin coated
